message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 12
Recent Green phases: added pin tests for 4 runes (no block), 7 runes (175, also pins round-up 197.5→198), 2 runes + 1 moonstone (different types no block), 3 runes + 3 moonstones (two blocks). No implementation changes were needed — all passed with existing componentGroupPremium / countByType / componentsPremium logic.

Refactor while keeping all tests green. The implementation is unchanged since the last refactor (basePremium decomposed into isComponent/sum/countByType/mainItemsPremium/componentsPremium). Evaluate whether anything can be simplified/clarified; document if none warranted. Run `pnpm test`.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 12 tests pass. Now let me evaluate the implementation.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Reviewing each name against its purpose:

- `componentGroupPremium(count)` — computes premium for a group of alike components. Clear and accurate.
- `isComponent(item)` — checks if item is rune/moonstone. Clear.
- `sum(values)` — reduces array to total. Clear.
- `countByType(items)` — produces a Map of type→count. Clear.
- `mainItemsPremium(items)` — sums base premiums of non-component items. Clear.
- `componentsPremium(items)` — sums component group premiums. Clear.
- `basePremium(items)` — total of main + components. Clear.
- `roundUpToWhole(value)` — ceils with float guard. Clear.
- `runScenario(scenario)` — entry point. Clear.

All names reveal intent accurately. No rename warranted.

## Step 2: APP Mass — Current Code

The recent green phases added no implementation code. The structure is already well-decomposed. Let me scan for refactoring opportunities across the four rules.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** Names are clear. One small observation: `mainItemsPremium` and `componentsPremium` both partition `items` by `isComponent` — `mainItemsPremium` filters `!isComponent`, `componentsPremium` filters `isComponent`. This is a conceptual split that reads clearly. No clarity gain available.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):** Looking for duplication. The `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` lookup is the only main-item pricing. The component pricing lives in `componentGroupPremium`. No duplicated knowledge.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** Let me examine `basePremium`. It's a one-line composition called once in `runScenario`. It is a meaningful named abstraction (the "base premium" concept the spec repeatedly references), so it earns its place.

Let me look carefully for a genuine micro-improvement. In `componentsPremium`:

```ts
const componentsPremium = (items: Item[]): number =>
  sum([...countByType(items.filter(isComponent)).values()].map(componentGroupPremium));
```

This is a dense one-liner: filter → countByType → spread Map values → map → sum. It's correct but packs four operations into one expression. Per Rule 2, intent could be made slightly clearer, but the current form reads left-to-right as a pipeline and each step is named. Spreading into intermediate variables would add bindings (mass) without meaningfully improving clarity — the operations are already named functions.

Let me compute APP mass for the densest function to assess whether decomposition helps.

**`componentsPremium` current mass:**
- Binding `items`: 1
- Invocation `sum`: 2
- Invocation `countByType`: 2
- Invocation `filter`: 2
- Binding `isComponent` (ref): 1
- Spread/`.values()`: 2
- Invocation `.map`: 2
- Binding `componentGroupPremium` (ref): 1
- Loop (the `.map`): 5

Total ≈ 18. Adding an intermediate `const componentCounts = ...` would introduce a binding (+1) and an assignment-like declaration without reducing any operation. Mass would rise, clarity would be marginal. Not warranted.

Let me check whether there's any actual improvement to be made by examining the one candidate that stands out: `roundUpToWhole` uses a magic `1e6`.

## Step 3: Apply Refactoring

The one genuine improvement candidate is the magic literal `1e6` appearing twice in `roundUpToWhole`. This is a Rule 2 (reveals intent) opportunity: the raw `1e6` does not explain *why* — naming it as a precision factor clarifies the float-noise guard, and naming it also removes the duplicated literal (Rule 3, minor). This is a small but legitimate clarity improvement that does not raise complexity.

Let me apply this single change.

content.text:
All 12 tests still pass.

## Refactoring Complete

**Naming Evaluation (first priority):** Reviewed every identifier (`componentGroupPremium`, `isComponent`, `sum`, `countByType`, `mainItemsPremium`, `componentsPremium`, `basePremium`, `roundUpToWhole`, `runScenario`). All names accurately reveal intent given the 12 tests now passing. No rename warranted — the decomposition from the prior refactor remains coherent and the recent green phases (rune/moonstone pin tests) added no new behavior to re-name around.

**Refactoring Applied (Rule 2 — Reveals Intent, with a minor Rule 3 benefit):**
- Extracted the magic literal `1e6`, which appeared twice in `roundUpToWhole`, into a named constant `FLOAT_GUARD_PRECISION`, and extended the comment to explain it snaps to 6 decimal places.
- This clarifies *why* the multiply/divide dance exists (float-noise guard) and removes the duplicated literal.

**APP Mass impact:** Neutral-to-trivial. The change replaces two constant literals (`1e6` ×2, mass 2) with two bindings (mass 2) plus one new constant declaration (mass 1) — a +1 nominal increase. Per the Four Rules, Rule 2 (clarity) trumps APP, and this is a clear readability gain for negligible mass cost. No conditionals, loops, or assignments were added.

**Rules 3 & 4 evaluation:** No other duplication exists (main-item pricing lives only in `BASE_PREMIUM`, component pricing only in `componentGroupPremium`). No superfluous elements — `basePremium` is a meaningful named concept from the spec even though composed of two helpers. The dense one-liner in `componentsPremium` (filter → countByType → values → map → sum) was considered for decomposition but left as-is: each step is already a named function and adding intermediate bindings would raise mass without improving clarity.

**Tests:** All 12 passing (33 todo remain for future cycles).

File changed: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_16-29-33_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
